transcript.transcript: Now, Pat, we talked about the fact that you were hired at the Coeur d'Alene Press in 1948. Could you tell us a little bit about how long you were there and your steps through the time frame and where you lived as you worked the 40 years for the Hagedon Corporation? Okay, I started in 1948 as an advertising salesperson, And 1953, I believe that Burrow took me to Wallace and his Buick, which impressed me of course. And just to talk to him, that was one nice thing about Burrow, he would get you away from the plant and be friendly and talk to you and take you to lunch. And we went to Wallace to see, They just purchased the North Idaho News and that's what they changed to, the Wallace paper. And Bob Blow was the advertising manager at the Corlaine Press. So on the way, Burrell said, do you think you could handle the duties of the advertising manager? And I said, well, absolutely, there's no way that I couldn't. So he said, well, the job's yours then. So I was made advertising manager, which I, that was my goal in life, was to be advertising manager at the press. And so then, Burrell became ill with cancer and Duane came back from Moscow and came in as an advertising salesman and was very energetic and very, and you know how Duane is and anything he does he's gonna do the very best job he possibly can. And what year was that? I, I, I, 58 I would say. Okay. Because his dad died in 59. if I'm not mistaken. So then Burrell made Dwayne publisher when he became real ill and became incapacitated, probably latter part of 58, first part of 59. Then when Burrell died, immediately, I think I could have been publisher, but if I'd been more forceful, that Duane made me business manager and still retained the title of publisher, the title of publisher and that he moved over into Burroughs offices over, I believe, at Thurden and Sherman, which is now the FNB Bank building. And that was in 61. Dwayne came back. He had the offices over there, but he brought the offices back. And we remodeled the press building again at that time. He came back into the press building. The press building, right. And then Don Kline came to work as the treasurer of the Hagedon Corporation, or Hagedon Newspapers at that time. Now my dates might be off a little bit, but we're pretty close. But it does give us a framework. And if you want we can check into it. And then in 63 they offered me the job of publisher at Kalispell. So that was your first job as publisher? No, Chick and I went to Kalispell and she didn't like it too well. I think the people there were quite forceful and They had a real excellent publisher there, but I think I could have done as well as he did, but he went to Klamath Falls as publisher, so they needed somebody there. But Chick didn't like it, so I came back and told Duane that, no, we decided that we'd stay in Coeur d'Alene. So immediately, we went up there in November. January 1st, he made me publisher of the Coeur d'Alene Press. I see. In 73. So then, 63. Then I was publisher for 10 years and 73, or January of 74, I decided to send me to Beloit and brought Roy back here, Roy Wellman. So I was in Beloit for about a year and a half and Dwayne called one July day and asked if I would be interested in going to Kalispell. And we discussed it not very long. We always made our decisions probably too hurriedly, but we wanted to get back to the families, etc. So we went to Kalispell and we moved. We bought a house and sold our house and moved and we're in Kalispell, the family, before school in less than a month. And what year again was that? That was 75. Okay. So we were the only unemployed about a year and a half. But it was an excellent community. We lucked out, had one of the nicer homes that we've ever had. We did that in Kalispell too. We've been very fortunate in finding homes. Now, Beloit is part of the chain of newspapers that Hagedorn still owns. For our listeners' sake, When did the 30-odd numbers of papers that Burl was associated with, when did that end and this other group of newspapers begin? That was the Scripps League, as I mentioned, and that was dissolved in 1976. And then Duane took so many papers into Hagedon and he changed the name to Hagedon Newspapers. And I'm not going to get into the names, I think I can remember most of them. That's fine, we can cover the last details. And that was in 76 and I was at Kalispell at that time. Some of them left and stayed with Scripps and some of us became part of the Haggedon newspaper chain. I feel that was an important point to cover. So now as far as your career goes, you moved back to Kalispell, and how long did you stay there? Okay, from 75 to 87. I was there 12 years, back later that month. And then in 1987... 87 came back here to publish here and Roy went over to Kalispell, Roy Wellman. And what year, in what year did you retire? 88. Okay. And that was Dwayne's plan to happen. I didn't really believe that he would do that, but that was his plan. So you weren't quite ready to retire? No, I don't think that. I think he was wise. I think he was smart in what he did, because I think I was probably overwhelmed to a certain extent with what was going on. Problems, I thought, in people and lawsuits, etc., which I think was probably stressing me out. And I think Duane could see it. Maybe he was... Maybe he saved my life. Uh-huh. Some kind insight there, perhaps on his part. Right. I'd like to backtrack just a little bit, Pat, and ask you how you and your father differed in your journalism and in your approach perhaps to journalism? I believe my father probably, well I know he did when he was younger, was, was his main philosophy was, was writing and editorializing in his newspaper. And one area that I just found out not too long ago that he was an advocate of the Indians' rights and the Kootenai Indians, and I think that came back to be one of his strong points. In fact, he did have, and I mentioned before that he was involved in farmland, and he did lease quite a bit of farmland from the Kootenai Indians and the tribe. And he was always a backer of the Indians. I remember when we, he and the Indians and the squads would come in the office about the same time as I did and ask him for some money and I'd be there secondly to try and get a nickel or a dime for a Coke or in those days that was a long many years ago in the early 30s. And so how do you differ as a... Well, I went into the business aspect of it as management. That was my goal, to be a manager of a newspaper. In fact, I had planned to take over, as I think I mentioned, the Montesquieu Herald from my father. Have there ever been times when you have thought about what it would have been like if the paper had still been in the family, how do you see that you would have published and managed that paper? Well I think I'd probably done a better job than my father and I think that we would, well, but times were better and everything was booming in Bonner's Ferry at that time. I think we could have led the community better than than was, well, than it was done. I shouldn't say that and somebody might hear that, but I think that we could have and we've probably been a maybe a big fish in a little pond. Chick was very happy that we never did live in Bonners, but we still have lots of friends there and family. Would you have expanded, perhaps become an owner of more than one paper? I don't know. I probably would if I had the opportunity. There was a paper that I always had my eye on was the Libby Western Montana News. That was a good paper. And why did that paper attract you? I don't know, it was just a good paper and Libby was a community about the same as Sandpoint and had a lot to offer at that time. I think it's still and it's right kind of in a chain right up the Kootenai River from Bonner's Ferry. And Troy was really wasn't a factor. That was a small town. But they had a paper at one time. In what ways do you see the similarities and differences in philosophy as far as running a paper between Burl and Duane? Well I think they're more in the same vein as I was of management aspects. I hope I'm saying it right, that they were in management instead of editorial writing. And Burrough was a advertising manager at the Corleone Press before he bought out, or bought Scott, I think he bought Scott out and then became a partner with a Scripps League group. But Dwayne was somewhat the same and I think he was trained in that manner, although he was more aggressive than Burl, as far as I'm concerned and more forward I guess that might be. We're always, you know, it was a little bit quiet and I mean he got things done the way that he wanted but I mean he wasn't I didn't step out and like Dwayne does. Dwayne is energetic and and really goes after what he wants. Right. How have you seen journalism generally change in the past 40 years since you started out in the field, not specifically in the area of Coeur d'Alene, but just journalism in general? Well, the biggest thing, as I mentioned to you previously, is the advent of the offset printing. And that just changed journalism in so many ways. And Duane was one of the pioneers in that field and just did marvelous things with Offset. And we had a very small press and of course you know the review and all the big papers, metropolitan papers became offset printing method users. After the Coeur d'Alene arrested? We were the first in the Northwest. For our listeners who really don't understand the mechanics of journalism, can you explain a little bit to us what method was used before offset, and then specifically what offset is? Well, hot metal was the big use, and what you would do is, they used linotypes and this is still going to be tough to explain to anybody, but they had mats and each one of them had a letter on it, and then it went up and then the hot lead was shot into these and created a letter, and they were brought down and they were placed by a compositor into the, I can't even think of the name, but it was a steel brace that held all the type together. And then you printed off a flatbed press with the rollers that would roll over and the paper would come down and then you'd push the paper onto the inked type and you'd get an image. You get an image. Now with the offset, it's all photo onto an aluminum sheet. And then it's taken a picture and then it's on to the aluminum sheet and then you have a acid bath that brings out the photo the same as you would on a photograph on a negative. And so that's the process that's used right now? I didn't explain that very well. That's fine. I kind of caught you off guard there, but that sounds like it was perhaps one of the major changes. And do you remember what year that took place in? 63, here in Coeur d'Alene. Why do you suppose Duane was so enthusiastic about making that change? Well I think as I mentioned Les Wolfe got interested in it. And what was his capacity? Well, he was the production manager for Duane, for all the papers that Duane was involved in with Scripps. And I think I mentioned that he was general manager for several of them, for the Scripps League. Who was general manager? Duane. Oh, OK, yes. And so that Les kind of talked him into it, I presume. I wasn't there on all occasions, but Les was just, this was his baby. And he did a tremendous job with it. And he worked day and night to perfect it. And it was, it was a, probably a less expensive means of producing a newspaper, and you had much better production. The pictures were far superior to letterpress and it made a cleaner product. So there wasn't so much ink rubbing off onto the... No, ink. Yet some people feel that the offset there is more ink, but really there isn't. What kind of changes have you seen specifically in the in the Coeur d'Alene press in the last 40 years? Well that was the largest one and of course then that involved in reducing the size of the paper. And as I said, the reducing the size of the paper was made it possible for us to produce more pages at less cost, so we could put more into the paper. The photography was much better, and so the reproduction of pictures and photography was exceptional, and was color, and we became an advocate of the use of color news and a color ink and process color which became very important and as you see that now they have two or three or four process color pictures in all the newspapers in the area. And I think it will become expanded in the future and probably become very much used. And I think the newspapers are going to become quite a bit like the magazines are today. And I'm an advocate of that. I think the advertising, put the advertising at the top of the page and just do all kinds of different things that that'll make the reader read advertising articles, features, and etc. I don't see why we can't be the same as a magazine and only come out daily. Are you saying that, would it become like a magazine in that it would be published fewer times a week? No, no daily, but in that format, in the same format as a magazine. As you see, maybe they might have advertising at the top of the front page actually in some of magazines, and articles are given more of a flair, I guess, is what I want to say. Then we can do the same thing. So, in a way, are you saying that newspapers is heading more in this direction from the days when it was short to the facts only, please, and less of the feature and the flair? What do you think has brought on this change? Is it competition with television news or the change in the reader? Or What has brought this change about? Change of the reader, I think. What do you see the reader is looking for? Well, they're younger, and they like bright things. I happened to be out at the mall yesterday, and I'm like, gosh, I just can't believe it. I didn't believe I was that old. But I mean, the bright clothes and neon colors and shorts. And I thought the girls had passed that phase of the mini skirts, and my gosh, they're minier than they were when we were, everybody was upset with the miniskirts, you know, but I mean it's just, and they're training, I mean the merchant is training the people and the young people what to buy. That's the way I see it, and we can do the same thing, or the newspaper can. What paper now in the Coeur d'Alene area would you say perhaps proposes the or is the competitor with the Coeur d'Alene Press? Well, I'd have to say the spokesman review is definitely. And do you have a philosophy or a viewpoint as to when that competition really started gaining momentum and how it has affected the Coeur d'Alene press? Well I would say maybe five, eight years ago I think that they made that decision that they were going to come into North Idaho. I don't think they really went out against Coeur d'Alene primarily, but I think North Idaho, and they have been here for 50 years or more anyway. But not under those circumstances by putting in an office right in Coeur d'Alene and they have one in Sandpoint. So they have physically made their presence known in that way? Right. And I believe that they have as staffed as many editorial people as the Coeur d'Alene papers. They do not sell advertising very much, actually. All they're trying to do is provide this news product. And I presume that they sell their advertisers in Spokane, that fact that they're bringing the paper to those people to draw them into Spokane, which I don't, I disagree with. I don't believe that's right. But then I probably fought them for 40 years, too. How do you see that it perhaps changed or affected the product of the Cordeline Press? Well, any time you have competition, you try and meet it or beat it. And normally, we always, when I was in management, it was our thought to beat it, not even meet it, do a better job. I think that's what you have to do. What advice would you give to any future journalists who might hear this tape? I don't know what to say on that, Julie. I don't know exactly how you mean it. Well, should they concentrate, for example, as you have, on management, or should there be emphasis on a broader base of education so that they can see not just their community but the world in general and how things relate? Or should they specifically focus in to one area of journalism? Just any thoughts on any of those subjects? I would say like you mentioned, broader base, but, and the younger people are, have a greater learning capacity or a greater chance to learn than we did, I hope. But I would say that they should get every bit of education they can in several fields. I would always wished I'd taken accounting and law, which I think would have helped considerably in what I was doing, but I think you have to be, you have to know what, be able to write, and one thing that I didn't take that I should have taken was public speaking. But then, and if you get into management you do have to do quite a bit of public speaking. And there again, Duane does a good, excellent job at the public speaking. I've always been frightened of it. You've done a wonderful job on this. What about the area of computer science? Do you see a need to get into that in relationship to journalism? It's a great asset to journalism. As myself, or anybody that's going to go into management, I'd stay out of it and let somebody else do it. Because hey, it takes time and that's the, as I see it and I haven't been too much involved in it because I got out just at the beginning of it. It takes a lot of time and you're spending a lot of money with those people to do that type of work and they like it and you know it's kind of like a child's play. I mean, they're putting these things on the screen, and then they transfer them into the newspaper. And it's just like you used to do when you were a kid, with a drawing board. Are the computer services used for information and research in the newspaper business, or are your main sources mainly the AP and UPI? It's just AP primarily, and then of course the other wire services, the feature services, what I should say. UPI is practically extinct. Pat, I have one last question I would like to ask you, and that is, how would you best want to be remembered as a journalist? I'd like to best be remembered as a journalist as taking care of people, and I'm a people person and I think that that's the most important asset that newspapers have, is people working for them and covering people's lives. And I think that when you retired that that was really a popular feeling about you that you in fact did do that during your career. Well I hope I did. Thank you very much for the time and the memories that you have shared and and we appreciate everything that you've shared with us, Pat. Thank you, Julie. You're doing a wonderful job. This is a lot of work. Thank you.

lines.line: > The speaker began his career at Coeur d'Alene press in 1948 as an advertising salesperson. By 1953, he was taken to Wallace by Burl, the then-manager, which marked a significant step in his career. Impressed by the management, he was soon promoted to advertising manager, a position he had aspired to. His career progression continued as he took on more responsibilities, eventually leading to a pivotal role in the company.

metadata.title: Career Beginnings and Advancements
metadata.synopsis: The speaker began his career at Coeur d'Alene press in 1948 as an advertising salesperson. By 1953, he was taken to Wallace by Burl, the then-manager, which marked a significant step in his career. Impressed by the management, he was soon promoted to advertising manager, a position he had aspired to. His career progression continued as he took on more responsibilities, eventually leading to a pivotal role in the company.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses his transition to the role of publisher, initially at Kalispell and later at Coeur d'Alene press. Despite challenges, including his wife's dissatisfaction with Kalispell, he made significant decisions that shaped his career trajectory. His leadership roles expanded over the years, culminating in his appointment as publisher at Coeur d'Alene press, where he served until his retirement.

metadata.title: Evolving Roles and Leadership
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses his transition to the role of publisher, initially at Kalispell and later at Coeur d'Alene press. Despite challenges, including his wife's dissatisfaction with Kalispell, he made significant decisions that shaped his career trajectory. His leadership roles expanded over the years, culminating in his appointment as publisher at Coeur d'Alene press, where he served until his retirement.

lines.line: > The speaker reflects on the major changes in the newspaper industry, particularly highlighting the shift from traditional printing methods to offset printing. This technological advancement significantly improved the quality and efficiency of newspaper production. The speaker credits Dwayne, a colleague, for pioneering this change, which had a profound impact on the industry.

metadata.title: Industry Transformations and Technological Advances
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the major changes in the newspaper industry, particularly highlighting the shift from traditional printing methods to offset printing. This technological advancement significantly improved the quality and efficiency of newspaper production. The speaker credits Dwayne, a colleague, for pioneering this change, which had a profound impact on the industry.

lines.line: # Reflections and Advice for Future Journalists

> The speaker compares his journalistic approach to that of his father, noting a shift from editorial focus to management. He emphasizes the importance of a broad education for journalists, including skills in management, law, and public speaking. The speaker advocates for adaptability and a proactive approach in journalism, suggesting that future journalists should be prepared to embrace changes and innovations in the industry.

# Reflections and Advice for Future Journalists

> The speaker compares his journalistic approach to that of his father, noting a shift from editorial focus to management. He emphasizes the importance of a broad education for journalists, including skills in management, law, and public speaking. The speaker advocates for adaptability and a proactive approach in journalism, suggesting that future journalists should be prepared to embrace changes and innovations in the industry.
